text: Hi everyone, the video today will show the laparoscopic resection of a large rectosigmoid mass.
Preoperative endoscopy failed in this lady twice because of the presence of a very tight rectosigmoid stricture.
Although the mucosa looked normal, the stricture could not be negotiated.
A CT scan and a CT colonoscopy showed the presence of thickened and inflamed recto-sigmoid mucosa with a very tight structure.
Although a diagnosis of diverticular disease was made, malignancy could not be entirely ruled out.
The laparoscopic view reveals a very large recto-sigmoid mass accompanied by phlegmon, with inflamed and thickened tissue visible in the surgical field.
The surgical view reveals a large recto-sigmoid mass adhered to the left-sided tubovarian structures, peritoneum, and pelvic wall.
The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, carefully splitting the peritoneum to access the underlying structures.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric artery up to its origin from the aorta, carefully isolating the vessel from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and excises multiple lymph nodes surrounding the recto-sigmoid mass, removing them along with the specimen during the low anterior resection procedure.
The surgeon isolates and removes the apical lymph node, which is sent separately from the main specimen.
Following the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), the surgeon continues with the lateral to medial dissection approach, separating tissue layers in the pelvic region.
The surgeon separates the mesocolon from the retroperitoneal structures, following the fascia of Toldt direction, revealing the anatomical planes between these structures.
The ureter is visibly crossing over the left common iliac vessels, with the gonadal vessels clearly demonstrated in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to access the posterior total mesorectal excision (TME) plane while dissecting a large mass from the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon lifts the mass from the pelvic cavity, revealing a relatively bloodless plane, indicating successful development of the correct tissue layer.

As by this point, no preoperative pathology was available, the procedure was being carried out in an oncological fashion. The patient was fully made aware of the absence of a preoperative pathological diagnosis and consented to proceed with the procedure.
The surgeon develops the anterior plane between the rectum and the vagina, using a narrow DVR retractor inserted in the vagina to aid in the dissection.
The surgeon focuses on the left side of the surgical field after freeing the posterior and anterior planes, indicating a shift in attention to a more challenging area of the dissection.
The surgeon pulls the peritoneum into the phlegmon or rectal mass, performing an en bloc resection to ensure adherence to oncological principles.
The surgeon gently separates the tubo-ovarian structures from the phlegmon, as they are adhered by flimsy adhesions, allowing them to be removed without formal dissection.
The surgeon removes the section of the left pelvic wall and peritoneum adhered to the rectal mass, excising it en bloc in accordance with oncological principles.
The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection around the mid to lower rectum, preparing the area for the application of a linear cutter stapler.
The patient did very well post-operatively. The pathology showed this to be severe diverticular disease. No malignancy was found in the specimen.
Thank you for watching.
